title: Meggyleves
subtitle: Hungarian cold sour cherry soup
excerpt: Chilled sour cherries simmered in spiced red wine, finished with soured cream until silky and pale pink. A summer starter unlike almost anything else in European cooking.
sourceNotes: Meggyleves (sour cherry soup) is a Hungarian summer speciality, served cold as a starter at the beginning of a meal. It is characteristic of Hungarian cooking in combining what other cuisines would treat as dessert flavours (fruit, sweetness, cream) with a soup course. The soup is made with meggyes (sour cherries), not sweet cherries, and the sourness is what makes it work. The wine, spices, and cream are what transform it from a fruit compote into a soup.

recipe.freezeNotes: Do not freeze — the cream and cherries both suffer.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make the day before and refrigerate overnight for best results.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before serving and chilled overnight. The flavour deepens considerably with time.

content.text: Meggyleves is jarring the first time: a cold, sweet-sour, cherry-pink liquid with cream stirred through it, served at the start of a meal. It is not dessert. The soured cream cuts the sweetness; the wine and cinnamon add depth; the cherries are tart enough to keep the whole thing firmly in savoury territory. After one bowl, the idea makes complete sense.

Sour cherries are essential. Sweet cherries produce a cloying result with none of the tension that makes the dish work. Use fresh sour cherries when in season, or jarred Morello cherries the rest of the year. Drain the jar liquid and use it in place of some of the wine if it is not too sweet.

items.prepNote: sour (fresh stoned, or jarred Morello, drained)

content.text: Combine the {{cherries}}, {{red-wine}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, {{cloves}}, and 300 ml of water in a saucepan. Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 minutes until the cherries are tender and the liquid is lightly syrupy. Remove from the heat. Add the {{lemon-juice}} and the {{sea-salt-fine}}. Remove the cinnamon stick and cloves. Leave to cool to room temperature.

Whisk the {{soured-cream}} until smooth, then whisk in a ladleful of the cooled cherry liquid to loosen it. Stir the cream mixture back into the cherry soup. Taste and adjust sugar and lemon juice — the balance should be clearly sour-sweet, not just sweet. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight, until thoroughly cold. Serve in chilled bowls.

items.symptom: Soup tastes like dessert, not a starter
items.cause: Too much sugar, or cherries not sour enough
items.fix: Add more lemon juice to increase the acid balance. The soup should make you slightly pucker — if it is just sweet, it is not correct

items.symptom: Cream curdling or separating in the cold soup
items.cause: Cream added to warm liquid, or whisked too vigorously after chilling
items.fix: Add the cream only when the soup has cooled to room temperature. If it separates in the fridge, whisk gently to re-emulsify before serving

items.cause: Not enough cherry solids, or too much water
items.fix: Remove half the cherries, blend them to a smooth purée, and stir back in. This thickens the soup without adding starch

content.text: Meggyleves appears on Hungarian restaurant menus in June and July when sour cherries are briefly in season. It is unusual enough that it regularly surprises guests who are not expecting a cold fruit soup at the start of a meal, and good enough that they nearly always order it again. At home it is made for summer lunches and dinner parties when the cherries are right. The soup requires no skill and almost no time: the quality depends almost entirely on the sourness of the cherries and the generosity with the cream.
